trajectory_summary: The call opened with a genuinely engaged customer — bill pain was real, usage was rising, and Zachary was cooperative through the verify and bill-collection phases. Energy peaked around the bill discussion at 02:06 and held through the photo exchange. The call's shape turns at 07:51 when Zachary requests program information before committing, and Meg's partial concession on the email offer converts a near-firm booking into a conditional one. The appointment is tentative by the customer's own word, lacks the button-up handshake, and the email offer creates a cooling-off window that raises no-show risk.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a tentative appointment, not a confirmed sit. Zachary used the word 'tentatively' himself and conditioned the booking on his work schedule. Without the 'is that fair' handshake, the no-driving/computer anchor, or a firm time confirmation, this should be tagged as a fragile/conditional booking rather than a clean appointment. The comp model (paid on sits, not bookings) makes this distinction material — a no-show here does not pay Meg.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up protocol — converting tentative to committed
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Let's tentatively plan for Monday, you said, at 03:30?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Zachary set the 'tentative' framing and Meg accepted it without deploying a single element of the button-up protocol. On a call where the customer was cooperative and the bill pain was real, the appointment should have been firmer than it is. Given that Meg is paid on sits (not bookings), a no-show here costs her directly. The Monday morning confirmation call she promised is a partial recovery but not a substitute for the verbal handshake. This is the highest-leverage coaching gap on this call because it's the one that directly affects whether Meg gets paid.

key_moments.quote: I just wanna see what the options are now, compared to what they were then. Because I know that there were credits that were available then that I don't know if they're available now.
key_moments.stewart_read: Zachary hands Meg a rich interest_in_solar answer: bill pain, a prior pitch he regrets not acting on, and a specific concern about tax credits. The schema coaching note on this item is explicit — 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Bottom setters check the box and move on.' Meg spent roughly 15 seconds on it before pivoting to the bill question. The regret signal ('I just kinda regret not doing it then') was the emotional anchor of the entire call and Meg walked past it entirely.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. So the the big federal tax credits, those did end up going away.
key_moments.stewart_read: Two schema drift issues in the same 90-second window. First, Meg states the federal tax credits 'did end up going away' — the schema notes the credit may still be claimable for year-end installs and explicitly states Ion can no longer guarantee it but it remains possible. A flat 'they went away' is inaccurate and forecloses a potential value point. Second, at 03:01 Meg states the credit threshold as 650, not the schema-specified 670. Neither error is catastrophic on this call, but both are factual gaps that could create closer-handoff friction if Zachary raises either point again.

key_moments.quote: Mine is. I don't want it run until I'm ready.
key_moments.stewart_read: Zachary's credit-pull boundary is a trust signal, not an objection. He's telling Meg — and by extension the closer — that he wants to control the pace of commitment. Meg handled it correctly in the moment ('Yeah. I don't even have the pallet too') and moved on without friction. The coaching value here is in the handoff: the closer must know not to rush the credit pull or frame it as a routine step. Zachary will read that as a boundary violation.

key_moments.quote: I am seeing a tree in the front yard that's probably gonna put some shade on the roof. Is that still there?
key_moments.stewart_read: Meg is using Google Maps proactively before the call to assess site conditions — this is a credibility-building move that signals preparation and professionalism. It also surfaces a potential shading concern before the customer raises it. The follow-up at 00:56 ('if my design team looks at it and they say, hey. We might need to remove the tree to get more sun exposure. Is that something you're open to?') is a clean qualification question. Zachary's answer ('It is my tree, so yes') is an enthusiasm signal — he's already mentally willing to modify his property for solar. Meg acknowledged it but did not anchor on it.
